title: Kabab koobideh
subtitle: Persian ground lamb and beef kebab on flat skewers
excerpt: Iran's most iconic kebab: a mixture of minced lamb and beef kneaded with grated onion, allspice, and turmeric, pressed onto wide flat skewers in long cylindrical shapes and grilled over very high heat. The challenge is keeping them on the skewer, the mixture must be kneaded to the right texture so it grips the metal and doesn't fall off.
sourceNotes: Kabab koobideh (koobideh means 'pounded' or 'beaten' in Farsi) is the foundational Persian kebab, documented in Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) and Najmieh Batmanglij's 'Food of Life' (1986). It is traditionally cooked on wide flat metal skewers over charcoal. The technique of kneading the meat until it becomes sticky and cohesive is the essential skill of the dish.

content.text: The kneading is everything. The mixture must be worked for at least 10 minutes by hand, squeezing and pressing until it becomes sticky, smooth, and pulls away from your hands in strings. This develops the proteins in the meat, which is what makes the mixture grip the skewer and hold together over high heat. Undermixed koobideh falls off the skewer or cracks and breaks. The kneaded mixture should feel almost paste-like, not like a loose mince.

The mixture is pressed onto wide flat skewers in a long cylinder, about 3 cm in diameter. At home, if flat skewers are unavailable, wooden skewers soaked in water work reasonably well for a griddle-pan version. The kebabs need to be turned carefully with tongs and not pressed, pressing forces out the juices. Traditionally served over flatbread with grilled tomatoes, raw onion, and sumac.

Combine the {{lamb-mince}}, {{beef-mince}}, squeezed grated {{onion}}, {{allspice}}, {{turmeric}}, {{black-pepper}}, and salt in a large bowl. Knead firmly for 10 to 12 minutes, squeezing and folding until the mixture is completely smooth, sticky, and cohesive. It should pull away from your hands cleanly. Cover and refrigerate for at least 1 hour.

Divide the mixture into 8 equal portions. With wet hands, press each portion firmly onto a skewer in a long cylinder about 3 cm wide, pinching the ends to seal. Refrigerate on a tray for 30 minutes before cooking. Heat a griddle pan to maximum heat until smoking. Cook the kebabs for 5 to 6 minutes per side without moving, until deeply coloured with a slight char. Rest for 5 minutes. Serve sprinkled with {{sumac}} alongside flatbread, grilled tomatoes, and raw onion.

items.fix: Knead for a full 10 to 12 minutes until the texture changes. Squeeze all moisture from the grated onion before adding. Chill for at least 1 hour on the skewer before grilling
items.cause: Meat not kneaded enough or too much moisture from the onion
items.symptom: Koobideh falls off the skewer during cooking

items.fix: Let the meat come to fridge-cold temperature before kneading. Squeeze the grated onion very firmly in a cloth before adding
items.cause: Too much onion moisture or meat too cold when kneading
items.symptom: Mixture feels wet and loose even after kneading

items.fix: The salt is essential for protein development and flavour. Taste the raw mixture before shaping. It should be well seasoned. The allspice should be detectable
Press firmly and evenly around the skewer, smoothing out any cracks with wet hands. Refrigerating before cooking also helps the mixture firm up and hold together
items.cause: Mixture undermixed or pressed onto the skewer with gaps

content.text: Kabab koobideh is the defining dish of Iranian street and restaurant culture. It is the kebab that appears at every Persian celebration, eaten with flatbread and rice. The image of wide flat skewers arranged over a charcoal grill, the fat dripping and flaming, is central to Iranian food memory. In Tehran, kebab shops that specialise only in koobideh and barg are an institution. The dish has spread through the Iranian diaspora worldwide. The flat skewer version stays the standard in Iranian restaurants even outside Iran. The skill of kneading meat until sticky is passed from one generation to the next.
